title: Goat kid disbudding
subtitle: When disbudding is appropriate, why timing matters, and how to support the kid before and after.
excerpt: Disbudding is the removal of the horn bud in young goat kids before horns grow. It is a licensed procedure in the UK: only a vet can perform it using a disbudding iron under local anaesthetic. The keeper's role is to recognise the right window, book the vet promptly, and care for the kid in the hours before and after.
sourceNotes: DEFRA Code of Practice for the Welfare of Goats, 2019. AHDB, Welfare guidance for goat kids. Veterinary Surgeons Act 1966, Schedule 3 operations requiring veterinary authorisation.

glossaryTerms.definition: The destruction of the horn bud in a young goat kid or calf, usually by cautery, before the horn attaches to the skull. In goats in the UK, disbudding must be performed by a vet under local anaesthetic; it is not a procedure keepers can carry out themselves.

A small bony protrusion felt under the skin of a kid's head within the first few days of birth, at the site where horns will grow. The bud attaches to the skull at around 7-10 days of age; disbudding must happen before this.

content.text: A dairy goat housed with other goats or handled regularly by people is safer and easier to manage without horns. Horned goats in a group injure each other and can injure their keeper.

is the conventional solution, but it must be carried out within a narrow window and only by a vet in the UK. Knowing when to look for the bud and when to make the call determines whether the procedure is possible.

on the kid's head from day 2 of life. They are detectable as small lumps under the hair. The bud attaches to the skull at around 7-10 days; once attached, cautery disbudding becomes more invasive and risky. Book the vet as soon as you detect the buds, aiming for the procedure at 3-7 days of age. Males are generally done at the earlier end of the window as their buds grow faster.

The vet will inject local anaesthetic around each bud and apply a hot disbudding iron directly over the bud for several seconds, destroying the horn-forming tissue. The procedure takes a few minutes per kid. Some vets also clip the hair over the buds before arrival to speed the process; ask when you book. The kid should be held firmly by the keeper during the procedure.

Keep the kid with its dam and ensure it has fed that morning. A kid that has not fed is hypoglycaemic and less resilient to any procedure. Have the kid warm, dry, and accessible when the vet arrives. Clip the hair over both buds if the vet has asked you to.

Return the kid to the dam immediately. She will lick and settle it within minutes. The burn site scabs over within 24 hours. Keep the housing dry and clean to avoid infection at the wound site. Wipe away any discharge from around the burn with a clean damp cloth if it accumulates. A kid that is not feeding 4 hours after the procedure, or that is shivering and hunched, needs a vet check the same day.

Some breeds of goat are polled (naturally hornless) by genetics. Polled goats do not need disbudding, but breeding two polled animals together carries an increased risk of hermaphrodite offspring in certain breed lines. Ask the breeder whether your kids' sire is polled or horned.
